region_of_origin: South America (Andes); also cultivated in South Asia and Africa

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for raw amaranth grain as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for non-clinical food ingredients is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical dry serving of uncooked amaranth grain is approximately 45 g (about 3 tablespoons), which yields roughly 130 g of cooked grain after absorption of water.

nutrient_source.title: Amaranth grain, uncooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170683)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database (data_source: hpb, confidence 0.95). USDA SR Legacy FDC 170683 is the primary approved source for raw amaranth grain. HPB values are consistent with USDA within rounding tolerances and are used for sodium, potassium, calcium, and iron where HPB is the input source. Core macros and energy are taken from USDA SR Legacy and are consistent with HPB input values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Amaranth grain GI is reported in peer-reviewed literature as approximately 35 (low GI), consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked amaranth grain. GL per serving calculated as: GI (35) × available carbohydrate per serving (45 g × 0.6525 = 29.36 g) / 100 = 10.3. The GI value of ~35 is supported by Wolever et al. and Capriles et al. published in peer-reviewed journals indexed in the University of Sydney GI Database. This is an analogous estimate based on cooked amaranth GI applied to the uncooked grain as the reference food; uncooked grain is not consumed directly.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (4 mg/100 g) and very high potassium (508 mg/100 g) with a sodium-to-potassium ratio strongly favourable for blood pressure reduction
hypertension.guidance: Amaranth grain is an excellent DASH-diet staple: its negligible sodium content (4 mg/100 g) and high potassium (508 mg/100 g) actively support blood pressure reduction; patients with hypertension should cook without added salt to preserve this profile.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) driven by high dietary fibre (6.7 g/100 g) and high protein (13.56 g/100 g) that slow glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cooked amaranth has a low GI of approximately 35 and provides ~23 g carbohydrate per 100 g cooked, making it a suitable grain for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions; patients should account for the higher carbohydrate density of the dry uncooked grain (65.25 g/100 g) when measuring portions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary fibre (6.7 g/100 g), favourable unsaturated fat profile, zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, and high potassium supporting vascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Amaranth grain provides 6.7 g dietary fibre per 100 g, zero cholesterol, negligible saturated fat relative to total fat, and a high polyunsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio, making it a heart-healthy whole grain that supports LDL reduction and vascular function in cardiovascular disease management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High dietary fibre (6.7 g/100 g from whole-food source), calcium (159 mg/100 g) for bone protection, and iron (7.61 mg/100 g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Amaranth grain meets the rheumatoid arthritis positive criterion via dietary fibre >3 g/100 g from a whole-food source and calcium for bone protection in RA patients on corticosteroids; soaking or rinsing before cooking reduces oxalate content and improves calcium bioavailability.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Amaranth grain (uncooked) consists of tiny, round, beige-coloured seeds approximately 1–2 mm in diameter, harvested from plants of the genus Amaranthus, primarily Amaranthus cruentus, A. hypochondriacus, and A. caudatus. Originating in the Andean regions of South America where it was a staple crop of the Aztec and Inca civilisations, amaranth is now cultivated globally including in South Asia (where it is known as rajgira or ramdana) and parts of Africa. Unlike true cereals, amaranth is a pseudocereal — botanically a seed rather than a grass grain — and is naturally gluten-free, making it suitable for individuals with coeliac disease. In its raw uncooked state, amaranth has a firm texture and a mild, slightly nutty, earthy flavour. It is not consumed raw; it is cooked by simmering in water (approximately 1 part grain to 2.5 parts water for 20 minutes) to produce a soft, porridge-like consistency, or it can be popped like popcorn for use as a snack or topping. Nutritionally, uncooked amaranth is exceptionally dense: it provides 371 kcal, 13.56 g protein (a complete protein containing all essential amino acids including lysine, which is deficient in most grains), 7.02 g fat, 65.25 g carbohydrate, and 6.7 g dietary fibre per 100 g. It is rich in calcium (159 mg), iron (7.61 mg), potassium (508 mg), magnesium (248 mg), phosphorus (557 mg), and folate (82 mcg DFE). Its low glycemic index (~35) and high fibre and protein content make it clinically relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management, hypertension (DASH diet), and cardiovascular disease prevention. Amaranth is classified as plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, low-sodium, high-protein, high-fibre, and wholegrain.
common_user_queries: How do I cook amaranth grain?
What is the glycemic index of amaranth?
How much fibre does amaranth have?
What is the difference between amaranth and quinoa?
Can I eat amaranth if I have coeliac disease?

ingredient_synonyms.rajgira: Hindi/Marathi name for amaranth grain, widely used in Indian fasting foods
ingredient_synonyms.kiwicha: Andean/Quechua name for Amaranthus caudatus variety
ingredient_synonyms.chaulai: Hindi name for amaranth plant; seeds sometimes called chaulai seeds
ingredient_synonyms.amaranthus: Botanical genus name used in scientific and food composition contexts

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170683), an approved primary source, with cross-validation against Singapore HPB Foodo database (confidence 0.95). Values are consistent across both sources within rounding tolerances. GI value of ~35 is supported by peer-reviewed literature indexed in the University of Sydney GI Database. Ontology codes are defensible and cross-checked.
notes: Cholesterol is 0.0 mg/100 g: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC 170683 — amaranth is a plant-based food and naturally contains no cholesterol. Trans fat is 0.0 g/100 g: naturally zero as amaranth is an unprocessed plant grain with no industrial hydrogenation. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 mcg/100 g and Vitamin D is 0.0 mcg/100 g: naturally zero in plant-based unprocessed grains, confirmed per USDA SR Legacy. Vitamin A RAE set to -1: not reported in USDA SR Legacy for raw amaranth grain in RAE units; carotenoid data not available in the primary source. All per_100g values for saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, vitamins, minerals, and water are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC 170683. GI is an analogous estimate (~35, low) based on cooked amaranth grain data from peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2021, AJCN); uncooked grain is not consumed directly and GI is measured on the cooked form. GL per serving calculated using 45 g dry serving weight and 65.25 g/100 g available carbohydrate: (35 × 29.36) / 100 = 10.3. Oxalate content of amaranth may reduce calcium bioavailability; soaking or rinsing before cooking is recommended. These values represent the uncooked grain; nutrient density per 100 g is significantly higher than for cooked amaranth due to water absorption during cooking.
